stimulus: Poetry in Classical Tamil, the language of the early Sangam Age in southern India, relies on the convention of tinai, in which an outwardly literal scene—a specific landscape with its associated flora, animals, and human activities—is understood by readers to evoke a particular interior emotional state. For example, the kurinji tinai, named after a mountain flower that blooms only rarely, describes mountainous terrain at night but is understood to depict the union of lovers. Readers familiar with the convention recognize that the kurinji setting is not the central subject of the poem; the mountain landscape functions as a vehicle for representing a state of heightened romantic feeling.
stem: Which statement about the kurinji tinai is most strongly supported by the text?

answerOptions.content: It is the only tinai used by Tamil poets to evoke feelings of romantic union.
It is understood by knowledgeable readers as a representation of an emotional state rather than as a description of an actual landscape.
It is the most highly regarded tinai in Classical Tamil poetry because of its association with the rare mountain flower.
It has gradually replaced earlier conventions used by Tamil poets to depict mountainous landscapes.

rationale:
Choice B is the best answer. The text explains that the kurinji tinai describes mountainous terrain but is recognized by familiar readers as representing the union of lovers—an interior emotional state for which the landscape merely serves as a vehicle.

Choice A is incorrect because the text does not claim kurinji is the only such tinai. Choice C is incorrect because the text doesn’t evaluate the kurinji’s relative status. Choice D is incorrect because the text doesn’t describe historical replacement of earlier conventions.
